draft.introduction: At Crito 49a–d, Socrates establishes the principle that one must never do wrong, even in retaliation. This absolute moral claim—that injustice is always harmful to the soul—became a cornerstone of Platonic and later Christian ethics.

translation.text: We ought not to retaliate or render evil for evil to anyone, whatever evil we may have suffered from him. For doing evil to people is no different from wrongdoing. One must never do wrong, nor, when wronged, return wrong for wrong, nor, when one suffers evil, defend oneself by doing evil in return.
translation.original_text: οὐδενὶ τρόπῳ φαμὲν ἑκόντας ἀδικητέον εἶναι

translator_notes.note: This principle of non-retaliation stands in sharp contrast to the prevailing Greek ethic of helping friends and harming enemies. The early Church Fathers saw in it a pagan anticipation of Christ’s teaching to turn the other cheek.
